Manx: GOODY BLAKE AS HARRY GILL.
English: GOODY BLAKE AND HARRY GILL.
Notes: GOODY BLAKE AND HARRY GILL.

Manx: O cre ta’n oyr jeh, sleih my chree,
English: Oh what’s the cause of it?
Notes: OH! what's the matter? what's the matter?

Manx: Cre shoh t’er jeet er Harry Gill?
English: What is this that ails Harry Gill?
Notes: What is't that ails young Harry Gill?

Manx: Dy vel e eeacklyn snaggeree,
English: That his teeth chatter,
Notes: That evermore his teeth they chatter,

Manx: Snaggeree e eeacklyn sthill.
English: Chatter his teeth still.
Notes: Chatter, chatter, chatter still!

Manx: Son vestyn cha vel Harry feme,
English: For waistcoats Harry needs not,
Notes: Of waistcoats Harry has no lack,

Manx: Ny flannyn glass dy horçh erbee;
English: Or grey flannels of any sort,
Notes: Good duffle grey, and flannel fine;

Manx: Ta lhuishag lhiabbagh er e ghreeym
English: He has a bed blanket on his back,
Notes: He has a blanket on his back,

Manx: As cooatyn fit dy phlooghey nuy.
English: And coats fit to smother nine.
Notes: And coats enough to smother nine.

Manx: Geurey as sourey, oie as laa,
English: Winter and summer, night and day,
Notes: In March, December, and in July,

Manx: Te yn un chooid da Harry Gill,
English: ’Tis all the same with Harry Gill;
Notes: 'Tis all the same with Harry Gill;

Manx: E nabooyn t’ad ooilley gra
English: His neighbours they all say,
Notes: The neighbours tell, and tell you truly,

Manx: Dy vel e eeacklyn snaggeragh sthill.
English: That his teeth chatter still.
Notes: His teeth they chatter, chatter still.

Manx: Syn oie as moghrey as munlaa
English: In the night and morning and noon,
Notes: At night, at morning, and at noon,

Manx: Te yn un chooid da Harry Gill;
English: 'Tis all the same with Harry Gill;
Notes: 'Tis all the same with Harry Gill;

Manx: Ta grian as eayst yn un chooid da.
English: The sun and the moon are all the same to him
Notes: Beneath the sun, beneath the moon,

Manx: She snaggeragh ta e eeacklyn sthill.
English: ’Tis chattering is teeth are still.
Notes: His teeth they chatter, chatter still!

Manx: She eirinagh va Harry roish shen,
English: Harry was a farmer before then,
Notes: Young Harry was a lusty drover,

Manx: Agh grayse! Cre’n guilley lusty v’eh,
English: But grace! What a lusty lad he was,
Notes: And who so stout of limb as he?

Manx: Lesh lieckanyn cha jiarg as roseyn,
English: With cheeks as red as roses,
Notes: His cheeks were red as ruddy clover;

Manx: As lesh coraa cha ard as three.
English: And his voice as loud as three.
Notes: His voice was like the voice of three.

Manx: Shenn Ghoody Blake va ee boght as thanney
English: Old Goody Blake was was poor and thin
Notes: Old Goody Blake was old and poor;

Manx: Cha row eck bee ny eaddagh mie,
English: She had no food or good clothing
Notes: Ill fed she was, and thinly clad;

Manx: As shoh dooyrt sleih myr v’ad goll shaghey,
English: And this is what people said as they went by,
Notes: And any man who passed her door

Manx: “Jeeagh cre’n mucklagh moal dy hie.”
English: “Look what a miserable pigsty of a house.”
Notes: Might see how poor a hut she had.

Manx: Feiy-laa va ee snieu er slane y çhiaghtyn,
English: All day she spun for the full week,
Notes: All day she spun in her poor dwelling:

Manx: As tammylt dy arrey-ny hoie [MS. “dy oirane ’syn oie.”
English: And a spell of the night watch [MS. ‘of hours at night’]
Notes: And then her three hours' work at night,

Manx: Agh moal yn faill v'ec yn ven voght shen,
English: But how miserable the wage that that poor woman had,
Notes: Alas! 'twas hardly worth the telling,

Manx: Cha ren eh geeck son cainlyn j’ee.
English: It didn’t pay for candles for her.
Notes: It would not pay for candle-light.

Manx: Va’n ven shoh beaghey ayns kione-jiass Hostyn.
English: This woman lived in the Southern End of England.
Notes: Remote from sheltered village-green,

Manx: Yn thie eck heose ayns breast y clieu,
English: Her house up in the breat of the mountain,
Notes: On a hill's northern side she dwelt,

Manx: Va geayl feer deyr roish row e roshtyn,
English: Coal was really expensive before it was arriving,
Notes: Where from sea-blasts the hawthorns lean,

Manx: Son ve çheet voish foddey voue.
English: For it was coming from far away from them.
Notes: And hoary dews are slow to melt.

Manx: Ny cheayrtyn jees dy henn vrasnaghey
English:
Sometimes a couple of old agitators[1]
[1] vrasnaghey] though not found eslwehere as a noun, here is certainly is a noun (in dual case). The assumed meaning is ‘agitators / provokers, etc.’, based on the verb / adjective [brasnaghey] ‘provoking, urging, etc.’
Notes: By the same fire to boil their pottage,

Manx: Son nagh beagh ad ayns wheesh dy feme,
English: So they wouldn’t be in so much need,
Notes: Two poor old Dames, as I have known,

Manx: ’Syn un thie cooidjagh veagh ad beaghey,
English: In the same house together they would live,
Notes: Will often live in one small cottage;

Manx: Agh ish, ben voght, v’ayns thie ee hene.
English: But she, a poor woman, was in her own house.
Notes: But she, poor Woman! housed alone.

Manx: Ny yeih ’sy tourey veagh ee gennal,
English: Yet in the summer she would be happy,
Notes: 'Twas well enough when summer came,

Manx: Feer vie va'n traa shoh er e son,
English: This time would be really good for her,
Notes: The long, warm, lightsome summer-day,

Manx: Va ee soie, çheu-mooie jeh'n dorrys singal,
English: She sat outside the door singing,
Notes: Then at her door the 'canty' Dame

Manx: Va yn coraa eck myr y lhon.
English: Her voice was as harmonious as a blackbird.
Notes: Would sit, as any linnet, gay.

Manx: Agh tra harragh rioee as sniaghtey,
English: But when ice and snow would come,
Notes: But when the ice our streams did fetter,

Manx: Ny shenn chraueyn eck va craa,
English: Her old bones were shaking,
Notes: Oh then how her old bones would shake!

Manx: As er y chooyl va sleih goaill boght jeh,
English: And straight away people took her to be poor,
Notes: You would have said, if you had met her,

Manx: Dy drogh da Goody Blake yn traa.
English: That times were bad for Goody Blake.
Notes: 'Twas a hard time for Goody Blake.

Manx: Va’n fastyr hene j’ee doogh dy mie,
English: Even the evenings were pretty dire for her,
Notes: Her evenings then were dull and dead:

Manx: Agh[2] smooinee shiu nagh treih va cooish,
English:
O think how sad a case it was 
[2] Agh] taken here to be [Ugh] — an interjection of disappointment, frustration or defeat (Cregeen).
Notes: Sad case it was, as you may think,

Manx: Tra she feer feayr va’ee goll dy lhie,
English: When very cold, she was going to bed,
Notes: For very cold to go to bed;

Manx: Agh fud-ny-hoie ’sy lhiabee dooisht.
English: But all night in bed awake.
Notes: And then for cold not sleep a wink.

Manx: Ve jannoo boggey j’ee ’sy gheurey,
English: It made her glad in the winter;
Notes: O joy for her! whene'er in winter

Manx: Yn traa va yn gheay vooar sheidey wheesh,
English: When the great wind blew so much,
Notes: The winds at night had made a rout;

Manx: Ny biljyn ass ny fraueyn reuyrey
English: The trees from the roots were uprooting,
Notes: And scattered many a lusty splinter

Manx: As ny banglaneyn ock va brisht.
English: And their boughs were broken.
Notes: And many a rotten bough about.]

Manx: Ny yeih cha row eck, çhing ny slane,
English: Yet never had she, sick or well,
Notes: Yet never had she, well or sick,

Manx: Wheesh ayns stoyr, ta sleih dy ghra,
English: So much in store, people say,
Notes: As every man who knew her says,

Manx: Dy vrasnaghyn çhymsit traa shen
English: Of gathered sticks then,
Notes: A pile beforehand, turf or stick,

Manx: Va fit dy yannoo ee three laa.
English: That was fit to do her three days.
Notes: Enough to warm her for three days.

Manx: Agh tra veagh rio as lane dy feayraght,
English: Oh, when there would be ice and a lot of cold,
Notes: Now, when the frost was past enduring,

Manx: Cre’n pian veagh ayns ny craueyn eck,
English: What a pain would be in her bones,
Notes: And made her poor old bones to ache,

Manx: Row red erbee veagh lieh cha cleaynagh
English: Was there anything that would be lying so alluring
Notes: Could any thing be more alluring

Manx: As shenn chleiy drine da Goody Blake?
English: As an old thorn hedge to Goody Blake?
Notes: Than an old hedge to Goody Blake?

Manx: Agh eisht ny cheayrtyn, sleih ta ginsh,
English: And then sometimes, people tell,
Notes: And, now and then, it must be said,

Manx: Tra va yn feayraght farraght’ sthill,
English: When the cold was still persisting,
Notes: When her old bones were cold and chill,

Manx: Va ec faagit aile as lhiabbee neesht
English: The fire and bed as well she was leaving,
Notes: She left her fire, or left her bed,

Manx: As goll gys cleiy lesh Harry Gill.
English: And going to one of  Harry Gill’s hedges.
Notes: To seek the hedge of Harry Gill.

Manx: Va Harry gourys car ny bleeaney
English: Harry was suspecting all year,
Notes: Now Harry he had long suspected

Manx: Yn maarlys shoh jeh Goody Blake,
English: Goody Blake of this theft,
Notes: This trespass of old Goody Blake;

Manx: As loo dy ghoaill urree cooilleeney
English: And swore to take revenge upon her
Notes: And vowed that she should be detected--

Manx: Tra veagh ee goit, as brasnag eck.
English: Whe she’ be caught, with a stick.
Notes: That he on her would vengeance take.

Manx: Veih keeil-çhiollee ve e chliaghtey
English: From (the) fireside it was his habit,
Notes: And oft from his warm fire he'd go,

Manx: Magh ayns feayraght goll dy ghleck
English: Out in coldness going to seize,
Notes: And to the fields his road would take;

Manx: Fud ny hoie ayns rio as sniaghtey
English: All night in ice and snow,
Notes: And there, at night, in frost and snow,

Manx: Son dy haartyn Goody Blake.
English: For to catch Goody Blake,
Notes: He watched to seize old Goody Blake.

Manx: Agh keayrt dy row cooyl thurran arroo
English: But once, behind a rick of barley,
Notes: And once, behind a rick of barley,

Manx: Va Harry jeeaghyn magh myr shoh
English: Harry was looking out thus
Notes: Thus looking out did Harry stand:

Manx: Ve mullagh eayst traa shen dy jarroo,
English: The moon was full then indeed,
Notes: The moon was full and shining clearly,

Manx: As yn thalloo creoi dy rio
English: And the ground hard with ice
Notes: And crisp with frost the stubble land.

Manx: T’eh clashtyn sheean, t’eh er e chassyn,
English: He hears a sound, he’s all awake,
Notes: He hears a noise — he’s all awake

Manx: Roie myr fer veagh ass e cheeayl.
English: Running like a crazy man.
Notes: Again? on tip-toe down the hill

Manx: T’eh Goody Blake dy baghtal r’akin,[3]
English:
’Tis Goody Blake clearly visable,
[3] dy baghtal r’akin] ‘clearly visible’. The MS gives [dy baghtal fakin] ‘seeing clearly’,
Notes: He softly creeps ’tis Goody Blake;

Manx: Ta ee ec cleigh lesh Harry Gill.
English: She’s at a hedge owned by Harry Gill.
Notes: She’s at the hedge of Harry Gill!

Manx: Yn boggey v’er ve harrish common,
English: His joy was exceptional,
Notes: Right glad was he when he beheld her:

Manx: Brasnag lurg brasnag myr v’ee ec thie.
English: Stick after stick as though she was at home,
Notes: Stick after stick did Goody pull:

Manx: Follit v’eh cooyl billey-tramman
English: Hidden, he was behind an elder tree,
Notes: He stood behind a bush of elder,

Manx: Choud as veagh ee ghoaill e saie.
English: Whilst she’d be taking her fill.
Notes: Till she had filled her apron full.

Manx: Hyndaa ee eisht son dy gholl thie,
English: She turned then to go home,
Notes: When with her load she turned about,

Manx: Bundeil dy vrasnagyn va eck
English: A bundle of sticks she had,
Notes: The by-way back again to take;

Manx: Tra lheim e magh myr dooinney keoie,
English: When he jumped out like a madman,
Notes: He started forward, with a shout,

Manx: As haare eh greim er Goody Blake.
English: And caught a grip of Goody Blake.
Notes: And sprang upon poor Goody Blake.

Manx: Son haare eh hold j’ee eck çhyndaa,
English: For he caught a hold of her as she turned,
Notes: And fiercely by the arm he took her,

Manx: As chum eh ee myr dooinney keoie.
English: And held her like a madman.
Notes: And by the arm he held her fast,

Manx: Dy dewil yn roih eck ren eh craa
English: Cruelly he shook her arm
Notes: And fiercely by the arm he shook her,

Manx: As geam, “t’ou taarit aym ny yeih.”
English: And yelled, “I’ve caught you after all.”
Notes: And cried, "I've caught you then at last!"--

Manx: Agh Goody cha ren fockle gra,
English: But Goody didn’t say a word,
Notes: Then Goody, who had nothing said,

Manx: Agh hug ee raad da e bundeil
English: But let her bundle go
Notes: Her bundle from her lap let fall;

Manx: Ayns padjer ren ee eisht hyndaa
English: In prayer she then turned
Notes: And, kneeling on the sticks, she prayed

Manx: Gys Jee ta harrish ooilley reill.
English: To God who rules over all.
Notes: To God that is the judge of all.

Manx: Padjer myr shoh ren ee y ghoaill
English: A prayer, thus she prayed,
Notes: She prayed, her withered hand uprearing,

Manx: As Harry ren ghoaill tastey jeh,
English: And Harry he took heed of it,
Notes: While Harry held her by the arm--

Manx: “O Yee, ta harrish ooilley reill,
English: “O God, who rules over all,
Notes: "God! who art never out of hearing,

Manx: Nagh lhig uss da ve arragh çheh!”
English: Don’t you let him be warm ever again!”
Notes: O may he never more be warm!"

Manx: Yn eayst va feayr erskyn e kione.
English: The moon was cold above her head
Notes: The cold, cold moon above her head,

Manx: Yn traa va Goody prayll myr shoh.
English: The time when Goody prayed thus.
Notes: Thus on her knees did Goody pray;

Manx: Ghow Harry tastey jeh ny goan
English: Harry took heed of the words
Notes: Young Harry heard what she had said:

Manx: As hie eh roish cha feayr as rio.
English: And he went (left) as as cold as ice
Notes: And icy cold he turned away.

Manx: V’eh goll as gaccan laa ny vairagh
English: He was going and grumbling the next day
Notes: He went complaining all the morrow

Manx: Dy row eh gennaght feayraght sthill
English: That he was still feeling cold
Notes: That he was cold and very chill:

Manx: E chree va çhing, e eddin groamagh.
English: His heart was sick, his face gloomy.
Notes: His face was gloom, his heart was sorrow,

Manx: Ah treih yn laa da Harry Gill!
English: Alas! That day for Harry Gill!
Notes: Alas! that day for Harry Gill!

Manx: Yn laa hie cooat mooar er wheesh,
English: That day a big coat went on him so much,
Notes: That day he wore a riding-coat,

Manx: Agh cha ghow eh çhiass erbee,
English: But he didn’t get any warmth
Notes: But not a whit the warmer he:

Manx: Jerdein lurg shen cheu eh jees,
English: The Thursday after that he wore two,
Notes: Another was on Thursday brought,

Manx: As roish y doonaght cheau eh three.
English: And ere the Sabbath he wore three.
Notes: And ere the Sabbath he had three.

Manx: Cha row e agh fardail ny yeih,
English: It was only in vain though;
Notes: 'Twas all in vain, a useless matter,

Manx: Dagh lhiushag myr va currit da,
English: Each blanket as was given to him,
Notes: And blankets were about him pinned;

Manx: Va e eecklyn bwoilley noi ry hoi
English: His teeth were striking against each other,
Notes: Yet still his jaws and teeth they clatter;

Manx: Myr uinnag lesh y gheay veagh craa.
English: Like a window would shake with the wind
Notes: Like a loose casement in the wind.

Manx: Ren Harry coayl e eill myr shoh
English: And Harry lost his flesh like this;
Notes: And Harry's flesh it fell away;

Manx: Dy row dagh nane gaoill tastey jeh,
English: So everyone took heed,
Notes: And all who see him say, 'tis plain,

Manx: Cre erbee choud as veagh eh bio
English: However long as he would be alive
Notes: That, live as long as live he may,

Manx: Nagh bee eh dy bragh arragh çheh.
English: He never will be warm again.
Notes: He never will be warm again.

Manx: Cha vow shiu voish coraa ny sheean,
English: You will not get a voice or sound from him,
Notes: No word to any man he utters,

Manx: Cha loayr eh veg rish beg ny mooar.
English: He won’t speak a word to small or large,
Notes: A-bed or up, to young or old;

Manx: Agh taggloo kinjagh t’eh rish hene
English: Only constantly talking to himself
Notes: But ever to himself he mutters,

Manx: “Ta Harry Gill boght feayr dy liooar,"
English: “Poor Harry Gill is cold enough,”
Notes: "Poor Harry Gill is very cold."

Manx: Ny lhie ny girree, laa ny oie,
English: In bed or rising, day or night;
Notes: A-bed or up, by night or day;

Manx: E eeacklyn snaggeraght t’ad sthill.
English: His teeth still chattering they are.
Notes: His teeth they chatter, chatter still.

Manx: Nish smooinee, shiuish erinee,
English: Now think, ye farmers,
Notes: Now think, ye farmers all, I pray,

Manx: Er Goody Blake as Harry Gill.
English: About Goody Blake and Harry Gill.
Notes: Of Goody Blake and Harry Gill!

Manx: [1] vrasnaghey] though not found eslwehere as a noun, here is certainly is a noun (in dual case). The assumed meaning is ‘agitators / provokers, etc.’, based on the verb / adjective [brasnaghey] ‘provoking, urging, etc.’
[2] Agh] taken here to be [Ugh] — an interjection of disappointment, frustration or defeat (Cregeen).
[3] dy baghtal r’akin] ‘clearly visible’. The MS gives [dy baghtal fakin] ‘seeing clearly’,
